species: Old-Growth in Windstorm -- full 400-year canopy roar, old-growth Pacific Northwest, windstorm season
scientific_name: Pseudotsuga menziesii (Douglas-fir) + Thuja plicata (Western Red Cedar) + Picea sitchensis (Sitka Spruce) + Acer macrophyllum (Big-Leaf Maple) + Alnus rubra (Red Alder) -- multi-species old-growth canopy, wind as acoustic driver
sound_type: Full 400-year old-growth canopy roar in Pacific frontal windstorm. Sustained 50-70 km/h winds, gusts to 80-100 km/h (Beaufort 8-10). Frequency spectrum: 20-80 Hz structural creaking (Douglas-fir trunk bole, root movement); 100-500 Hz branch flexion and major branch turbulence; 500-2,000 Hz canopy movement, Western Red Cedar mid-frequency pendulous branch wash; 2,000-8,000+ Hz needle and leaf sound, high-frequency wind component. Amplitude: 70-85 dB mid-frequency sustained, gust modulation 10-15 dB variation at 30-120 second intervals. Multi-layer canopy (60-80m dominant, 30-50m sub-canopy, 10-20m understory) all in simultaneous motion. E=7 through sustained enveloping complexity rather than single-impulse maximum.
ecosystem: Old-growth Pacific Northwest forest, coastal/lowland type. 400+ years age. Multiple canopy layers. Large-diameter snags. Pacific Northwest old-growth reduced 80-90% from pre-contact extent by logging. Remaining habitat: Olympic National Park (Hoh, Queets, Quinault, Bogachiel rainforest valleys; 140-200 inches annual precipitation; densest North American biomass north of tropics), Mount Rainier National Park, North Cascades National Park, select national forest stands and tribal lands. Old-growth vs. plantation: multi-layer vs. single-layer; massive individual trees vs. small diameter; snags vs. none; structural complexity vs. uniform. These produce fundamentally different windstorm acoustic events.
season: Windstorm season: October-March. Pacific Northwest windstorm season driven by procession of North Pacific low-pressure systems. Atmospheric river-associated storms most intense. Columbus Day Storm (October 1962, gusts 100+ mph) represents catastrophic extreme. Degree-338 event is non-catastrophic maximum: sustained 50-70 km/h with gusts to 100 km/h. Above 130 km/h gusts: event shifts to catastrophic with tree falls dominating -- qualitatively different from E=7 sustained canopy roar.
family: Meteorology (wind as acoustic driver). Multi-species forest acoustic resonators: Pseudotsuga menziesii (Order Pinales, Family Pinaceae), Picea sitchensis (Order Pinales, Family Pinaceae), Thuja plicata (Order Cupressales, Family Cupressaceae), Acer macrophyllum (Order Sapindales, Family Sapindaceae), Alnus rubra (Order Fagales, Family Betulaceae).
milestone: FULL 400-YEAR CANOPY ROAR. The acoustic output of a complete old-growth forest in major windstorm -- a sound that requires 400 years of biomass accumulation to produce. Old-growth loss of 80-90% means this sound is only audible in the remaining 10-20% of Pacific Northwest old-growth. Each Beaufort-scale windstorm in intact old-growth is an event documentation. Olympic Peninsula rainforest (Hoh, Queets, Quinault, Bogachiel): the densest North American biomass north of tropics, 140-200 inches annual precipitation, Sitka Spruce up to 3m diameter at 400+ years.

coordinates.description: Representative: Hoh Rainforest, Olympic National Park, Jefferson County WA. 47.86°N 123.93°W. Intact coastal old-growth with Sitka Spruce, Western Red Cedar, Douglas-fir at 400+ years. 140+ inches annual precipitation. Most intact coastal old-growth in contiguous US. Also: Queets, Quinault, Bogachiel rainforest valleys. Cascades old-growth: Ohanapecosh (Rainier), Carbon River (Rainier), old-growth stands Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF.

acoustic_data.wind_parameters: Sustained 50-70 km/h (Beaufort 8-10, gale to storm); gusts 80-100 km/h. Non-catastrophic maximum: maximum canopy motion without structural failure at scale. Gust cells produce 10-15 dB amplitude modulation at 30-120 second intervals. Crown sway at 400-year tree scale: 2-8 second oscillation period, 2-4 meter amplitude at crown.
acoustic_data.frequency_analysis: 20-80 Hz: Douglas-fir trunk creaking, root movement structural; 100-500 Hz: branch flexion and major branch turbulence; 500-2,000 Hz: canopy movement, Western Red Cedar pendulous branch mid-frequency wash (most textured acoustic contribution per unit volume); 2,000-8,000+ Hz: needle and leaf high-frequency, wind component. Amplitude by band: structural 60-75 dB; mid-frequency 70-85 dB; high-frequency 65-80 dB.
acoustic_data.species_contributions: Douglas-fir: deep bass structural creaking 20-80 Hz + crown hiss 2,000-5,000 Hz. Sitka Spruce: singing branch droops most distinctive coastal windstorm voice, flexible structure max sway. Western Red Cedar: most textured 500-2,000 Hz mid-frequency wash from pendulous lacy branch fans. Big-Leaf Maple: seasonal variation (bare winter vs. leafed spring). Red Alder: brittle trunk flexion sound, riparian component. Snags: structural groaning under load, potential fall events.
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: Sustained enveloping complexity rather than maximum-impulse E=8-9. Always loud (70-85 dB mid-frequency), never at maximum-impulse level (no single-peak event like thunder crack or large waterfall). The temporal variation (gust modulation) prevents monotony without producing an impulse peak. E=7 through sustained moderate-to-high energy: 400 years of biomass, all in motion, producing a continuous multi-frequency roar.

nodes.title: 400-Year Biomass Accumulation as Acoustic Mass
nodes.description: The old-growth windstorm sound requires 400 years of growth to produce: massive individual trees with low-frequency structural resonance, multi-layer canopy producing simultaneous frequency coverage, large-diameter snags. No younger forest produces the same acoustic event in the same wind. Age = acoustic mass.

nodes.title: Pacific Northwest Old-Growth Loss -- 80-90%
nodes.description: Pacific Northwest old-growth reduced 80-90% from pre-contact extent by 20th century logging. Remaining 10-20% primarily in national parks (Olympic, Rainier, North Cascades). The degree-338 sound is increasingly rare because its acoustic substrate is rare.

nodes.title: Beaufort 8-10 Windstorm -- Non-Catastrophic Maximum
nodes.description: E=7 is the non-catastrophic maximum windstorm level: Beaufort 8-10, maximum canopy motion without structural failure at scale. Above this threshold the event shifts to catastrophic (tree falls dominating), which is a qualitatively different acoustic event. E=7 is the sustained maximum before catastrophe.

cross_references.relationship: E=7 + E=7 = E=14. Both carry the weight of historical depth: 400 years of biomass accumulation (forest) and 1985-1992 proto-metal groundwork (band). Sustained structured heaviness rather than maximum impulse.
E=6 single-species crown gale vs. E=7 full multi-species old-growth canopy. Multi-layer structural complexity adds one energy unit above single-species crown gale.
E=9 to E=7. Summit energy drops from tri-layer apex convergence to sustained forest roar. Maximum simultaneous forces vs. maximum sustained resonance.
